item.title: Microsoft 365 MFA Bypass, Jamf Salesforce Exposure, and Circles Surveillance Exports
item.description: This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by how users, vendors, governments, and analysts operationalize them. Mitiga detailed an attempted business email compromise campaign against a Microsoft 365 organization in which an executive account was accessed from multiple geographic locations, including Singapore, Dubai, and San Jose, California. The initial compromise used an adversary-in-the-middle phishing technique that gave the attacker access to the executive’s account and mailbox. The notable persistence mechanism was the registration of a second Microsoft Authenticator app for the compromised user. Mitiga reported that Microsoft 365 did not require an MFA re-challenge when authentication methods were changed in the Security Info section if the existing session token had already satisfied MFA, allowing the attacker to weaken the account’s effective protection after initial access. Jamf separately disclosed that a cybersecurity incident at third-party vendor Klue allowed an unauthorized party to access data in Jamf’s Salesforce instance through Klue’s integration. Jamf said its products and customer service operations were not affected, that it disabled the integration after notification, and that it had no evidence of lateral movement. The company warned that contact information stored in Salesforce could be used in phishing campaigns impersonating Jamf employees or IT professionals. Cisco Talos used its newsletter to emphasize that possession of accurate security information does not reliably translate into secure decisions. The article framed security behavior as a human and organizational problem shaped by budgets, workloads, priorities, and operational constraints, even when established controls such as segmentation, backups, MFA, and control validation are well understood. The same Talos roundup referenced several credential- and access-focused security headlines, including ShinyHunters claiming a Council of Europe compromise, a credential-harvesting operation affecting more than 30,000 Fortinet devices, and Phantom Stealer activity targeting browser credentials. Together with the Mitiga and Jamf items, these reports show continued attacker focus on identity systems, browser-stored secrets, SaaS data stores, and post-compromise opportunities for phishing or persistence. The Record reported on Human Rights Watch findings that Bulgaria approved export licenses from 2018 through 2023 allowing surveillance firm Circles to sell products to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in countries described as repressive. Approved destinations reportedly included El Salvador, the United Arab Emirates, Serbia, Azerbaijan, Guatemala, Bahrain, Jordan, Malaysia, Morocco, and Panama. The tools identified in the report included Pixcell, which can capture internet data, phone calls, and messages; Landmark, which can geolocate mobile phones in real time; and Voice Over Location Enabler software, which can remotely intercept incoming and outgoing voice calls using the SS7 protocol. Human Rights Watch said the records cast the European Union’s export control regime in a harsh light, while Bulgaria stated that exports contradicting national, European, and international commitments are not allowed. The European Commission said member states remain responsible for dual-use export licensing decisions. Talos also described a reverse engineering workflow pairing local AI agents with tools such as the VB6 disassembler vbdec through a live COM interface. The approach allows analysts to issue natural-language prompts for tasks such as decompiling functions and building call graphs while keeping sensitive binaries on the local workstation. The discussion reflects a broader shift in malware analysis and security operations tooling, where AI-assisted workflows are being explored for analyst productivity without necessarily sending proprietary or sensitive samples to external services. In this case, the emphasis was on local orchestration of existing reverse engineering capabilities rather than cloud-based analysis.

item.title: NGINX CVE-2026-42530/CVE-2026-42055, Klue OAuth Theft, SocGholish Cleanup, and USB Clipper Campaign
item.description: Reporting in this 24-hour window centered on third-party access risk, active cybercrime infrastructure, critical edge-facing vulnerabilities, and abuse of trusted platforms for phishing and malware delivery. Several stories highlighted how attackers are using legitimate integrations, browser...
item.content:encoded:
Reporting in this 24-hour window centered on third-party access risk, active cybercrime infrastructure, critical edge-facing vulnerabilities, and abuse of trusted platforms for phishing and malware delivery. Several stories highlighted how attackers are using legitimate integrations, browser features, collaboration services, and removable media to bypass conventional security assumptions. Mitiga described an advanced Microsoft 365 business email compromise attempt in which an adversary-in-the-middle phishing flow enabled access to an executive mailbox and allowed the attacker to register a second Microsoft Authenticator app. The key persistence issue was that Microsoft 365 did not require a fresh MFA challenge when the attacker modified Security Info because the session had already satisfied MFA. The Klue incident expanded into a broader Salesforce data theft story. BleepingComputer reported that the Icarus threat actors used stolen OAuth tokens associated with Klue integrations to query customer Salesforce data via automated scripts, while Jamf said Klue’s integration allowed unauthorized access to Jamf Salesforce instance data and warned of potential phishing using CRM contact information. Other phishing and fraud reporting showed continued abuse of trusted brands and infrastructure. IRONSCALES detailed a legal-document phishing email that passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC while linking to mismatched infrastructure, and Silent Push tracked 227 FIFA World Cup-themed phishing domains tied to a consistent registration pattern attributed in an OCCIP alert to Ghost Stadium. Health-ISAC summarized FBI reporting on Silent Ransom Group, also known as Luna Moth, Chatty Spider, and UNC3753, which conducts data theft and extortion without traditional file encryption. In one reported 2026 campaign, the group allegedly escalated from remote access attempts to sending an actor posing as IT staff to a victim location to insert a storage device. Ransomware operations also affected operational technology-adjacent business processes. Mackay Sugar in Australia continued restoring systems after a cyberattack disrupted mill operations and harvesting, while the Gentlemen ransomware group claimed responsibility and threatened to publish allegedly stolen data. The ransomware ecosystem showed both maturation and stealthier tradecraft. Acronis reported that INC ransomware has claimed more than 830 victims since 2023 and has rewritten Windows and Linux/ESXi encryptors in Rust, while Symantec and Carbon Black reported DragonForce-linked use of Backdoor.Turn, a Go-based RAT that concealed C2 setup through Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure before establishing QUIC communications. F5 issued out-of-band fixes for two critical NGINX Open Source vulnerabilities: CVE-2026-42530, a use-after-free in the HTTP/3 QUIC module, and CVE-2026-42055, a heap-based buffer overflow affecting certain HTTP/2 proxying and gRPC configurations. Additional high-severity NGINX Gateway Fabric flaws involved authenticated injection of arbitrary NGINX configuration directives. Web supply chain compromise remained prominent. Zscaler reported that malicious SmartApeSG JavaScript was injected into the Okendo Reviews widget, using obfuscation, localStorage execution control, and staged loading before presenting fake CAPTCHA-style prompts that led users toward PowerShell or HTA downloaders. WordPress ecosystems featured both compromise and disruption. Wordfence found that ShapedPlugin’s paid plugin update flow distributed infected releases containing a malicious loader and fake hidden plugin designed to steal credentials and WooCommerce data, while Operation Endgame removed SocGholish malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites and took 106 servers and domains offline. Microsoft detailed a Windows cryptocurrency clipper campaign active since February 2026 that spreads through USB drives using malicious LNK files. The worm hides original documents, replaces them with matching shortcuts, creates scheduled tasks for propagation, uses Tor-based hidden-service C2, and monitors clipboard contents for seed phrases, private keys, and wallet addresses. The broader malware roundup included 23 deceptive Chrome extensions affecting about 758,000 users, abuse of Anthropic Claude shared chats and Google Ads to deliver MacSync, and malicious npm packages dropping the Rust-based NastyC2 framework. Cisco also updated its advisory for CVE-2026-20127, stating that the Catalyst SD-WAN flaw has been exploited as a zero-day since 2023 by UAT-8616. Apple patched CVE-2025-20701 in Beats Studio Buds firmware, a Bluetooth-range flaw that could allow microphone access on an unpaired device seeking pairing. Separately, India told a court that Telegram could not proactively police channels tied to alleged exam-paper leaks, a dispute that also produced cross-border disruption through a BGP route leak. Security teams continued experimenting with AI-assisted analysis workflows. Cloudflare described a model-agnostic vulnerability discovery harness that reduced more than 20,000 raw candidates to 7,245 actionable findings through staged validation, while SpecterOps described how BloodHound MCP reduced model context overhead by replacing many endpoint-specific tools with composite tools and MCP resources. AI governance and access risk were also recurring themes. The Hacker News highlighted orphaned AI agents and standing privileges as an emerging identity-management issue, while Cisco Talos discussed local AI-agent workflows for reverse engineering that keep sensitive binaries on analyst workstations. Human Rights Watch reporting on Bulgaria’s export licensing records added a surveillance technology dimension. The report said Bulgaria allowed Circles to sell interception and geolocation tools to multiple governments from 2018 through 2023, raising renewed scrutiny of European Union dual-use export controls.

Klue OAuth breach linked to 'Icarus' Salesforce data theft attacks — Must Read

Klue’s OAuth breach let the Icarus extortion group steal Salesforce CRM data from multiple customers by abusing Klue Battlecards integration tokens. Salesforce disabled the Klue connection, and affected organizations are urged to revoke tokens, rotate credentials, and review SaaS logs for suspicious API activity.

  • Klue suffered an OAuth breach that allowed the Icarus threat actors to steal Salesforce CRM data from multiple organizations in an ongoing extortion campaign.
  • Salesforce disabled the connection between the Klue Battlecards app and Salesforce while the security incident is investigated.
  • ReliaQuest said attackers used OAuth tokens associated with customer Salesforce instances and automated Python scripts to query Salesforce’s REST API for nearly 24 hours.
  • Huntress reported that Klue told customers attackers compromised Klue backend systems and pushed a malicious code update that stole OAuth tokens used for integrations.
  • Klue disabled integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, SharePoint, Zoom, Gong, Chorus, Clari, Google Drive, and Slack while responding to the incident.

DragonForce Hackers Abuse Microsoft Teams Relays to Hide Backdoor.Turn C2 Traffic — Must Read

DragonForce ransomware actors used a custom Go-based RAT, Backdoor.Turn, to hide C2 traffic through legitimate Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure during a months-long intrusion at a major U.S. services firm. The campaign combined stealthy relay abuse with BYOVD evasion and post-ransomware backdoor deployment to maintain covert access.

  • Threat actors associated with DragonForce ransomware used a custom Go-based RAT called Backdoor.Turn to conceal command-and-control traffic inside Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure.
  • Symantec and Carbon Black said Backdoor.Turn was deployed against a major U.S. services firm, where attackers remained on the victim network for between one and two months.
  • Backdoor.Turn obtains an anonymous Microsoft Teams visitor token backed by Skype identity services, uses a legitimate Microsoft TURN relay during connection setup, and then establishes a QUIC session to the attacker’s command-and-control server.
  • The attackers used a BYOVD technique with a Huawei driver named HWAuidoOs2Ec.sys to silence security software after launching a DLL side-loading attack from a ZIP archive disguised as a tech support hotfix.
  • Backdoor.Turn supports capabilities including command execution, process creation, network scanning, LDAP and Active Directory search, credential-based lateral movement, and browser credential theft.

Hot Take: Operation Endgame VS SocGholish — Must Read

Operation Endgame disrupted SocGholish, a long-running fake browser update malware framework used by TA569 to sell initial access to groups including EvilCorp, by taking down infrastructure and remediating compromised WordPress sites. Infoblox telemetry showed SocGholish reached nearly 55% of its cloud customer networks in early 2026, though only a small fraction appeared to progress to later-stage infection.

  • Operation Endgame disrupted SocGholish by taking down 106 servers and domains and remediating 14,971 compromised WordPress websites.
  • SocGholish is a fake browser update malware framework operated by TA569 that provides initial access to other cybercriminals, including EvilCorp.
  • Infoblox observed that nearly 55% of its cloud customer networks were exposed to SocGholish in 2026, but only a small number appeared to progress to the final stage of the attack.
  • SocGholish uses a multi-stage JavaScript framework involving traffic acquisition, traffic filtering, fake update prompts, and on-device JScript stager execution.
  • SocGholish uses domain shadowing for tier one and tier two hostnames, making its rapidly rotating infrastructure harder for defenders to identify and block.

F5 issues out-of-band patches for critical NGINX vulnerabilities — Must Read

F5 released emergency patches for critical NGINX flaws that can let unauthenticated remote attackers crash vulnerable servers or potentially execute code in certain non-default configurations. Administrators should update affected NGINX products immediately or apply F5’s mitigations if patching is not yet possible.

  • F5 released out-of-band security updates for multiple NGINX web server vulnerabilities, including two critical-severity flaws that could allow code execution on vulnerable systems.
  • The critical vulnerabilities are CVE-2026-42530 in ngx_http_v3_module and CVE-2026-42055 in ngx_http_proxy_v2_module and ngx_http_grpc_module, and they can be exploited by unauthenticated remote attackers on NGINX systems with non-default configurations.
  • Successful exploitation can cause a use-after-free or heap-based buffer overflow in the NGINX worker process, leading to a restart, and may allow code execution when ASLR is disabled or bypassed.
  • F5 issued fixes for affected NGINX products including NGINX Plus, NGINX Open Source, NGINX Gateway Fabric, and NGINX Instance Manager.
  • F5 also addressed two high-severity NGINX Gateway Fabric flaws, CVE-2026-11311 and CVE-2026-50107, that can let authenticated attackers inject arbitrary NGINX configuration directives.

SmartApeSG Launches Okendo Reviews Supply Chain Attack — Must Read

Zscaler ThreatLabz found that the widely used Okendo Reviews widget had been compromised to inject SmartApeSG JavaScript across e-commerce sites, creating a supply-chain exposure for potentially high-traffic storefronts. The malicious script used filtering and obfuscation to load later-stage ClickFix lures that can lead to RATs or information stealers such as NetSupport, Remcos, and StealC.

  • On May 14, 2026, Zscaler ThreatLabz identified a supply chain attack involving malicious JavaScript embedded in the Okendo Reviews widget, a customer review platform used by more than 18,000 brands.
  • The injected SmartApeSG JavaScript used localStorage-based execution control, User-Agent filtering, obfuscation, and staged retrieval to load additional content only under specific conditions.
  • The loader reconstructed next-stage infrastructure from XOR-obfuscated fragments, generated a randomized 8-character token, and dynamically inserted a new script element into the page.
  • The SmartApeSG infection chain commonly displays fake CAPTCHA or verification prompts, instructs users to run copied commands via the Windows Run menu, and retrieves PowerShell or HTA downloaders.
  • ThreatLabz reported the incident to Okendo, and Okendo confirmed it was aware of the security incident and restored the widget script to a clean state.

ShapedPlugin update flow hacked to infect WordPress sites — Should Read

ShapedPlugin’s official update system was compromised to deliver backdoored versions of three paid WordPress plugins, installing hidden fake WooCommerce plugins that steal credentials and site data. Affected admins should update to the fixed versions and rotate passwords, 2FA secrets, and other exposed credentials.

  • Multiple paid WordPress plugins from ShapedPlugin were compromised in a supply chain attack that distributed infected releases through the vendor's official update system.
  • The incident affected Product Slider Pro before 3.5.4 for WooCommerce, Real Testimonials Pro 3.2.5, and Smart Post Show Pro before 4.0.2.
  • Wordfence found that the infected plugins contained a malicious LicenseLoader.php file that downloaded a second-stage backdoor and installed it as a hidden fake plugin named woocommerce-subscription or woocommerce-notification.
  • The fake plugin attempted to steal WordPress login credentials, 2FA secrets, wp-config.php database credentials and authentication keys, administrator details, SMTP credentials, and WooCommerce order data from the past three months.
  • Researchers believe the compromise involved ShapedPlugin's build pipeline, while WordPress.org-hosted releases were confirmed to be clean.

Advisory: Persistent MFA Circumvention in an Advanced BEC Campaign on Microsoft 365 Targets — Should Read

Mitiga found that attackers using AiTM phishing against Microsoft 365 can bypass MFA and maintain persistence by registering a second Microsoft Authenticator app without a fresh MFA challenge. Organizations should not rely on MFA alone and should enforce device-based Conditional Access and monitor authentication method changes.

  • Mitiga investigated an attempted business email compromise campaign targeting Microsoft 365 organizations that used adversary-in-the-middle phishing for initial access.
  • The attacker gained access to an executive’s Microsoft 365 account and mailbox and then registered a second Microsoft Authenticator app without the user’s knowledge.
  • Microsoft 365 did not require a fresh MFA challenge when the attacker accessed the Security Info section and changed the user’s authentication methods because the session token had previously satisfied MFA.
  • Mitiga stated that registering the second Authenticator app gave the attacker persistence, allowing reauthentication with MFA after the original session expired or was revoked.
  • Mitiga recommended device-based Conditional Access, monitoring for new authentication method registrations, and treating unexpected-location sign-ins as potential indicators of compromise.

INC Ransomware Emerges as Major RaaS Threat in 2026 with 830+ Victims Since 2023 — Should Read

INC has rapidly grown into a major ransomware-as-a-service operation, claiming 830+ victims since 2023 as affiliates shifted from disrupted groups like LockBit and BlackCat. The group relies on common but effective tactics including exploiting unpatched edge devices, stealing Veeam credentials, abusing remote management tools, exfiltrating data, and deploying Rust-based encryptors across Windows and Linux/ESXi systems.

  • INC has grown from a nascent ransomware-as-a-service operation into one of the most prolific cybercrime groups in 2026, claiming at least 830 victims since August 2023.
  • Acronis reported that the disruption of LockBit and the shutdown of BlackCat created opportunities for INC to expand as affiliates migrated to alternative ransomware operations.
  • INC’s Windows and Linux/ESXi encryptors have been rewritten in Rust to support cross-platform development and resist reverse engineering.
  • INC affiliates use methods including spear-phishing, credentials bought from initial access brokers, exploitation of public-facing applications, credential dumping from Veeam backup servers, LOLBins, commercial remote management tools, and Rclone-based data exfiltration.
  • ZeroFox data showed that INC was the fourth most prominent ransomware group in Q1 2026, with more than 120 incidents during that period.

Health-ISAC Hacking Healthcare 6-18-2026 — Should Read

Health-ISAC highlights an FBI warning that Silent Ransom Group is escalating from phishing and remote access scams to physically impersonating IT staff to plug storage devices into victim computers, enabling privilege escalation and data theft. The key takeaway is that cybersecurity defenses must account for physical security and employee verification controls, not just remote threats.

  • Health-ISAC’s Hacking Healthcare examined the evolution of the Silent Ransom Group, which now attempts to gain physical access to victims’ systems to escalate privileges and exfiltrate data.
  • The FBI reported that Silent Ransom Group has been active as an independent entity since at least 2022 and is also known as Luna Moth, Chatty Spider, and UNC3753.
  • Silent Ransom Group conducts data theft and extortion operations without relying on traditional ransomware encryption, instead seeking rapid access, immediate data exfiltration, and extortion through threats of public disclosure or sale of stolen data.
  • In a campaign from January through May 2026, Silent Ransom Group targeted dozens of organizations across professional, legal, and financial services in the United States.
  • According to the FBI, when remote desktop access failed, Silent Ransom Group sent a threat actor to the victim’s location posing as IT staff to insert a storage device into the victim’s computer.

Microsoft Details Windows Clipper Malware Campaign Using USB LNK Worm and Tor-Based C2 — Should Read

Microsoft detailed a Windows crypto-clipper campaign spreading through malicious USB LNK files that hides documents, persists with scheduled tasks, and uses Tor-based C2 to steal clipboard data, screenshots, seed phrases, and private keys. Defenders should focus on behavioral detections around script engines, removable-drive LNK execution, clipboard monitoring, and unusual Tor or PowerShell activity.

  • Microsoft disclosed a Windows-based cryptocurrency clipper campaign that has targeted users since February 2026.
  • The campaign spreads through malicious Windows Shortcut files distributed via USB storage devices, with the worm hiding common document files and creating matching LNK files to trigger malware execution.
  • The clipper uses Windows Script Host and ActiveX-driven logic to launch a bundled Tor proxy, poll a hidden-service command-and-control server, and perform clipboard theft, screenshot exfiltration, and wallet-address substitution.
  • The malware creates scheduled tasks for persistence and exits if Task Manager is running to evade detection.
  • Microsoft recommended behavioral detections and mitigations such as disabling AutoRun and AutoPlay, blocking LNK execution from removable drives, restricting wscript.exe and cscript.exe, and reviewing clipboard-related and screen-capture behavior on sensitive devices.

Australian sugar producer works to restore operations as ransomware group claims attack — Should Read

Australia’s Mackay Sugar is restoring operations after a cyberattack shut two mills and disrupted harvesting, while investigating claims by the Gentlemen ransomware group that it stole data. The company has found evidence of external access but has not confirmed the group’s involvement.

  • Mackay Sugar said it is investigating claims by the Gentlemen ransomware group after a cyberattack disrupted its operations more than a week earlier.
  • The company said it found evidence that an external party accessed parts of its IT environment but has not attributed the incident to a specific threat actor.
  • The cyberattack forced Mackay Sugar’s Racecourse and Farleigh mills to suspend operations from June 10, halting harvesting across large parts of Queensland’s Mackay region.
  • Mackay Sugar said it continues to restore systems and expects some harvesting to resume this week.
  • Gentlemen claimed it would publish allegedly stolen data if a ransom is not paid but did not disclose what information it claims to have obtained or specify its demands.

Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says — Should Read

Human Rights Watch says Bulgaria approved exports of Circles surveillance tools from 2018 to 2023 to governments with records of repression, enabling phone interception, location tracking, and internet monitoring. The report argues the case shows serious failures in the EU’s export controls for spyware and surveillance technology.

  • Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records showing that Bulgaria allowed the surveillance firm Circles to sell technology to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in El Salvador, the United Arab Emirates, Serbia, Azerbaijan, Guatemala, Bahrain, Jordan, Malaysia, Morocco, and Panama from 2018 through 2023.
  • The report says Circles sold tools including Pixcell, which can capture internet data, phone calls, and messages, and Landmark, which geolocates mobile phones in real time.
  • Human Rights Watch said Circles also sold “Voice Over Location Enabler” software that uses SS7 protocol to remotely intercept targets’ incoming and outgoing voice calls and collect location data for both parties.
  • The article says the documents cast the European Union’s export control regime in a harsh light because the EU has struggled to control sales of spyware and surveillance technology beyond its borders.
  • Bulgaria told Human Rights Watch that exports contradicting national, European, and international commitments, including human rights protections, are not allowed, while the European Commission said member states are solely responsible for licensing decisions on dual-use exports.

Nintendo confirms data stolen in WebMD subsidiary cyberattack — Should Read

Nintendo of America confirmed that employee survey data was stolen through the third-party TinyPulse service, but said Nintendo’s own systems, customer data, and financial data were not compromised. The Shadowbyt3$ extortion group claims it stole about 1GB of data and demanded a $2 million ransom.

  • Nintendo of America confirmed that threat actors stole survey data from TinyPulse, a third-party service used internally for employee surveys.
  • Nintendo said its own systems were not compromised and that no personal customer or financial data was accessed.
  • Nintendo said the exposed data was limited to internal survey content involving a small subset of employees, with most information dating back several years.
  • The Shadowbyt3$ extortion group claimed it stole close to 1GB of Nintendo-related data and demanded a $2 million ransom.
  • Shadowbyt3$ claimed the stolen data included employee personal details, bank statements, W-9 forms, employee IDs, progress plans, and reports from 2016 to 2026.

npm v12’s Biggest Security Change: From Implicit to Explicit Trust — Should Read

npm v12 will shift package installs from implicit to explicit trust by blocking lifecycle scripts, Git dependencies, and remote URL dependencies by default unless approved. This should reduce common supply-chain attack paths, but developers still need strict version pinning and monitoring because attackers may target already-approved packages or move malicious code to runtime execution.

  • npm v12 will move package installation from an implicit trust model to an explicit trust model by blocking certain high-risk installation mechanisms by default unless they are explicitly approved.
  • The npm v12 controls target three mechanisms: allowScripts for lifecycle script execution, --allow-git for Git repository dependencies, and --allow-remote for remote URL dependencies.
  • JFrog says these three installation vectors were involved in approximately 53% of malicious npm attacks observed over the past year.
  • The article cites Shai-Hulud variants, easy-day-js, CanisterWorm, and PhantomRaven as examples of campaigns that abused lifecycle scripts, Git dependencies, or remote URL dependencies.
  • The article recommends that organizations using allowScripts or approve-scripts combine approvals with strict version pinning and re-evaluate trust during upgrades.

Telegram admits it couldn't police exam-leak channels, India tells court — Should Read

India told the Delhi High Court that Telegram was warned before a nationwide block over NEET-UG exam-leak channels and admitted it could not proactively detect them. Telegram argues it cooperated and says the ban is unlawful, while the block also disrupted access outside India due to a BGP route leak.

  • India's government told the Delhi High Court that Telegram was warned about two weeks before it was blocked and conceded it could not proactively detect channels selling leaked exam papers.
  • The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology received multiple complaints about Telegram being used for the alleged NEET-UG 2026 paper leak, and the National Testing Agency identified channels, groups, and bots circulating leaked material and exam-related fraud.
  • The government said it first raised concerns directly with Telegram and called Telegram representatives to a June 3, 2026 meeting instead of immediately blocking the app.
  • Telegram challenged the block in court, said it cooperated throughout, and argued that the ban is unlawful.
  • The Telegram block disrupted access beyond India, including in the UAE, through a BGP route leak, while the NEET-UG re-exam is scheduled for June 21 and the block is set to lift June 22 unless the court changes that first.

FIFA World Cup: Hunting 227 Phishing Domains With the Silent Push MCP Server — May Read

Silent Push says it used its MCP server with Claude to expand seed indicators from an OCCIP alert into a cluster of 227 World Cup-themed phishing domains linked to Ghost Stadium. The key takeaway is that shared patterns like favicon reuse and domain naming conventions can be turned into automated queries for daily detection and blocking of new fraud infrastructure.

  • An OCCIP flash alert about fraudulent World Cup ticket and merchandise sites tied the activity to Ghost Stadium, a Chinese-speaking cybercriminal group, and included seed domains for investigation.
  • Silent Push used its MCP server with Claude to investigate the seed domains, identify the larger cluster, and generate queries for tracking new infrastructure.
  • The phishing sites reused the same favicons and followed a consistent domain registration format using a two- or three-letter prefix followed by “26fifashop.site.top.”
  • On the first day of the World Cup, 227 new domains matching the identified pattern had already been registered.
  • Silent Push says the generated queries can be run in Context Graph or integrated into tools such as XSOAR and Splunk to deduplicate results, search logs, and update watchlists or blocklists.

The Law Firm Document That Linked to a Cleaning Company — May Read

A phishing email from a legitimate UAE law firm domain passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC while using a legal document-sharing lure to send recipients to an unrelated cleaning company subdomain likely used for credential theft. The key warning sign was not authentication failure but behavioral mismatch: hidden BCC delivery, mismatched domains, and a CTA destination unrelated to the claimed sender or document workflow.

  • A phishing email from t[.]manzoor@hhslawyer[.]ae impersonated a legal document-sharing notification and included a “Review Document” button for document #01179 related to legal consulting services, statements, and proposals.
  • The email passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC with compauth=100 and was sent through Google mail infrastructure with a valid DKIM signature under the hhslawyer[.]ae domain.
  • The “Review Document” button linked to access[.]genesiscleaningusa[.]com/workspace/, while the button’s HTML title attribute displayed supplier[.]coupahost[.]com/invoices.
  • The From address used hhslawyer[.]ae, the visible To header addressed t[.]manzoor@hhslawyers[.]com, and the actual recipient received the message via BCC.
  • Microsoft scored the message at SCL=5, and Themis flagged credential theft targeting VIP recipients at 57% confidence before quarantining four affected mailboxes.

Apple fixes Beats Studio Buds flaw that let hackers spy on conversations — May Read

Apple patched a high-severity Bluetooth flaw in Beats Studio Buds that could let nearby attackers listen through the earbuds’ microphone before pairing. Users should ensure their earbuds receive Beats Firmware Update 1B211 via a paired iPhone, iPad, or Mac.

  • Apple released Beats Firmware Update 1B211 to patch a high-severity flaw affecting Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds.
  • The vulnerability could allow an attacker within Bluetooth range to listen through the microphone of a device that is not yet paired and is actively seeking pairing requests.
  • Apple said the issue is in open source code and that Apple Software is among the affected projects.
  • The flaw, tracked as CVE-2025-20701, was discovered by Dennis Heinze and Frieder Steinmetz of ERNW GmbH in Airoha system-on-a-chip components.
  • ERNW researchers said chaining CVE-2025-20701 with CVE-2025-20700 and CVE-2025-20702 could let attackers hijack Bluetooth audio connections and issue commands to a phone using the Bluetooth Hands-Free Profile.

Build your own vulnerability harness — Optional

Cloudflare argues that effective AI-driven vulnerability discovery requires a model-agnostic orchestration harness, not standalone agents, with persistent state, cross-repo tracing, deduplication, adversarial validation, and human-reviewed fixes. The key takeaway is that the durable value is in the pipeline that filters noisy model output into verified, actionable security patches.

  • Cloudflare built a model-agnostic vulnerability research pipeline that treats LLMs as interchangeable components rather than relying on a single model or prompt.
  • The Vulnerability Discovery Harness separates work into stages such as Recon, Hunt, Validate, Gapfill, Dedup, Trace, Feedback, and Report, with persistent state stored in SQLite so runs can resume after failures.
  • Cloudflare uses a separate Vulnerability Validation System with a different model to deduplicate findings, judge production reachability, and generate fixes that require human review before merging.
  • The harness requires hunters to state a threat model and provide a working proof-of-concept test against the original codebase, while independent validators attempt to disprove each finding.
  • Cloudflare reported that 20,799 raw candidates from the discovery harness were reduced through validation and triage into 7,245 actionable findings for engineering teams.

BloodHound MCP, One Year Later: What I Learned About MCPs, Models, and Context — Optional

The author’s main lesson from rebuilding BloodHound MCP is that MCP design is really context design: smaller composite tools, recoverable errors, and on-demand domain resources make LLMs more effective than exposing every API endpoint as a separate tool. The new approach cuts token overhead dramatically and helps models reason about BloodHound data instead of getting bogged down in tool selection.

  • The first version of BloodHound MCP exposed much of the BloodHound API directly, but this created a large tool catalog that consumed model context and made tool selection harder.
  • The refactored BloodHound MCP reduced fixed MCP overhead from about 19,016 tokens to 5,721 tokens by replacing many endpoint-specific tools with a smaller set of composite tools.
  • Composite tools such as user_info use parameters like info_type to access multiple underlying BloodHound capabilities while keeping the top-level tool catalog smaller.
  • The updated MCP uses shared error handling so invalid composite-tool calls return valid options and allow the model to recover and retry.
  • The project moved detailed BloodHound Cypher guidance into MCP resources so the prompt can guide the model on when to load references instead of trying to teach all domain knowledge up front.

The Scripts on Your Checkout Page Are Now a PCI DSS Problem — Optional

PCI DSS v4.0.1 now requires merchants to inventory, authorize, verify, and monitor all scripts on payment pages because third-party checkout scripts can be abused for Magecart-style skimming. The article promotes Reflectiz as an agentless tool that helps meet these requirements and produce QSA-ready compliance evidence.

  • Modern checkout pages often load many third-party scripts, including analytics tags, tag managers, support widgets, and payment iframes, and any of these scripts can be turned into a skimmer.
  • PCI DSS v4.0.1 requirements 6.4.3 and 11.6.1 require organizations to inventory and authorize every payment-page script, prove script integrity, and detect tampering with page content and HTTP headers as received by the browser.
  • Integrity360 Europe, a PCI Qualified Security Assessor, reviewed the Reflectiz PCI DSS Platform and found it can effectively support compliance with PCI DSS requirements 6.4.3 and 11.6.1.
  • The article says Reflectiz monitors script behavior rather than only file hashes, deploys without code changes or snippets, and produces QSA-ready evidence with a full audit trail per page.
  • Since January 2025, merchants can exclude PCI DSS requirements 6.4.3 and 11.6.1 from SAQ A only if they confirm their site is not susceptible to script attacks.

Censys Expands Into Security Operations with Internet Intelligence-Powered Workflows — Optional

Censys announced an expansion into security operations with workflows and an Enrichment API that bring its real-time Internet intelligence directly into alert triage, investigations, and threat hunting. The goal is to help security teams enrich more alerts, prioritize risk faster, and automate decisions using external infrastructure context.

  • Censys announced its expansion into security operations to help organizations operationalize Censys Internet intelligence across security workflows.
  • The expansion builds on recent Censys innovations including risk scoring, adversary intelligence, AI-powered workflows, integrations with SIEM, SOAR, and threat intelligence platforms, and the new Censys Enrichment API.
  • Censys says its Internet intelligence is built on the Censys Internet Map, which it describes as a comprehensive and continuously updated view of global Internet infrastructure.
  • The new Censys Enrichment API is intended to bring real-time Internet context directly into alert triage, investigations, and threat hunting workflows.
  • Censys says security teams have used its Internet intelligence to identify emerging threats including a previously undocumented Russian remote access framework, exposed AI infrastructure, and Internet-connected critical infrastructure during heightened geopolitical activity.

Close Encounters of the Human Kind — Optional

The article argues that cybersecurity progress is less about simply knowing the right controls and more about accounting for messy human priorities, limited resources, and inconsistent behavior. It also highlights Cisco Talos research showing how local AI agents can automate reverse engineering workflows without sending sensitive binaries off-machine.

  • The article argues that having the right cybersecurity information does not necessarily lead organizations or individuals to take actions such as patching, using MFA, or practicing incident response.
  • The author says organizational decisions are shaped by limited budgets, workloads, competing business priorities, time, resources, urgency, and collective will.
  • Cisco Talos described a reverse engineering approach that pairs local AI agents with traditional tools such as the VB6 disassembler vbdec through a live Component Object Model interface.
  • The article says this local AI-agent approach can automate tasks such as decompiling functions and building call graphs while keeping sensitive binaries on the analyst’s workstation.
  • The newsletter highlights security headlines including ShinyHunters claiming a Council of Europe hack and a credential-harvesting operation compromising more than 30,000 Fortinet devices.

ThreatsDay Bulletin: Claude Chat Abuse, NastyC2 npm Packages, Device-Code Phishing + 25 More Stories — Optional

This week’s threats show attackers increasingly abusing trusted tools and platforms—AI chat links, browser extensions, OAuth device flows, coding agents, and npm packages—to deliver malware, steal credentials, and bypass defenses. The key takeaway is that “legitimate” infrastructure and workflows can no longer be treated as inherently safe.

  • A cluster of 23 deceptive Chrome extensions was found overriding users’ default search engines and routing queries through monetization brokers, affecting about 758,000 users.
  • Threat actors abused Anthropic Claude’s shared chat feature and Google Ads searches for AI developer tools to deliver the MacSync credential-stealing malware, with Asia-Pacific accounting for 67.2% of confirmed victims.
  • Three npm packages named node-ci-utils@2.1.4, win-env-setup@3.0.6, and macos-ci-utils@1.0.0 were found acting as droppers for the Rust-based NastyC2 post-exploitation framework.
  • Cisco updated its advisory for CVE-2026-20127 to state that the critical Catalyst SD-WAN flaw also affects Catalyst SD-WAN Validator and has been exploited as a zero-day since 2023 by UAT-8616.
  • CISA issued Binding Operational Directive 26-04 requiring federal civilian agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities within accelerated timelines based on factors including internet exposure and inclusion in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.

Orphaned AI Agents: How to Find Hidden Access Risks Inside Your Network — Optional

The article promotes an upcoming Hacker News/SailPoint webinar on the security risks of orphaned internal AI agents and standing privileges, where AI tools may keep accessing sensitive systems after their human owners leave. The key takeaway is that enterprises need visibility and ownership mapping for AI identities to revoke unnecessary access before it is abused.

  • The article says enterprises have accumulated administrative debt from orphaned AI agents and standing privileges as they rapidly adopt internal AI tools.
  • Orphaned AI agents are described as AI tools that remain active after their creator leaves the company, potentially retaining unmonitored access to sensitive databases and source code.
  • The article argues that traditional access tools can miss risks because they may not know whose identity an AI agent is using or whether its creator has left the company.
  • The webinar will cover the identity gap, methods for finding undocumented AI tools on a network, and ways to gain visibility into enterprise AI use without adding network infrastructure bottlenecks.
  • The Hacker News is hosting a technical briefing with SailPoint titled “Orphaned Agents & Standing Privileges: The Hidden Access Risks of Internal AI.”

5 reasons Microsoft 365 backup isn’t enough for business data protection — Optional

Microsoft 365 does not provide complete data protection on its own, leaving organizations responsible for backups, recovery, ransomware resilience, and compliance retention. The article argues that businesses should use third-party backup and security tools such as Acronis to fill those gaps.

  • Microsoft 365 operates under a shared responsibility model in which Microsoft ensures service availability and infrastructure security, while customers remain responsible for data protection, including backup and recovery.
  • The article says Microsoft 365’s native versioning and recycle bins provide only limited recovery against ransomware or malicious data loss, especially when encrypted or deleted files are synchronized across OneDrive or SharePoint.
  • Microsoft 365 retention policies may not meet long-term, flexible compliance requirements for industries such as healthcare, finance, and legal, because they are designed for governance rather than comprehensive backup.
  • The article states that Microsoft 365’s native recovery tools can make restoring specific files, emails, folders, Teams conversations, or user data time-consuming and inefficient.
  • The article promotes third-party solutions such as Acronis Cyber Platform as a way to add immutable storage, AI-based ransomware detection, granular recovery, long-term retention, and scalable management for Microsoft 365 data.

item.title: ChatGPT for Science Leak, AI Guardrail Gaps, and Executive Phishing Tradecraft
item.description: This digest window is dominated by two related themes: enterprise exposure around AI adoption and realistic social engineering tradecraft against senior leadership. Reporting highlights continued specialization of commercial AI offerings for research environments, while security commentary...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by two related themes: enterprise exposure around AI adoption and realistic social engineering tradecraft against senior leadership. Reporting highlights continued specialization of commercial AI offerings for research environments, while security commentary underscores gaps between provider-native AI controls and organizational SecOps visibility needs. Separately, an authorized assessment shows how public construction details and journalist impersonation can be used to drive executive engagement and downstream third-party exposure. BleepingComputer reported that OpenAI appears to be testing a new subscription and experience called ChatGPT for Science, based on references found in the ChatGPT web build. The article notes that availability remains unclear and that the offering may be restricted to verified institutes or universities rather than broadly available to all users. OpenAI already offers ChatGPT for personal, Teams, business, and enterprise customers, and recently announced GPT-Rosalind, a specialized model for enterprise-scale life sciences research delivered through a trusted-access deployment structure. The reporting reflects a broader trend toward domain-specific AI services for scientific and enterprise research workflows. In the life sciences context, specialized models and restricted deployment structures indicate that vendors are attempting to balance advanced research capability with controlled access models. The article does not state that ChatGPT for Science has been publicly launched, only that references in the web build suggest testing is underway. Netskope separately argued that native AI guardrails from providers such as Amazon Bedrock, Anthropic, and OpenAI often align more closely with provider liability and intellectual property protection than with enterprise SecOps requirements. The article states that these controls may block legitimate cybersecurity research while still allowing sensitive prompts or intellectual property to be sent to third-party providers. It also describes observability gaps where refusals or silent model behavior changes may not clearly identify which guardrail was triggered or why. NetSPI described an authorized social engineering assessment targeting a client’s executive leadership team. Testers used open-source research about a planned new facility to build a journalist impersonation pretext focused on alleged hazardous waste disposal at the construction site. The assessment used a ProtonMail account in a journalist’s name, a lookalike domain, and an Evilginx server designed to mimic the client’s Microsoft login flow and capture authentication data during the test. The scenario gained traction when an executive responsible for the construction project responded to the pretext. After the testers claimed there was a Teams issue, the executive received a phishing link and forwarded it to two contracting firms involved in the project. NetSPI stopped the phishing session and notified its point of contact because those contracting firms were outside the authorized engagement scope. The case illustrates how adversary-style pretexts can combine public business developments, executive responsibilities, and time-sensitive reputational concerns to increase credibility. It also shows how a phishing operation aimed at one organization can quickly propagate into adjacent vendors or contractors when trusted business relationships are involved.

item.title: Malicious VS Code Extensions, Crypto Clippers, CISA BOD 26-04, and Google IP Ad Targeting
item.description: This coverage window centers on trust boundaries that attackers and platform operators are increasingly exploiting or redefining: developer tooling, reputation systems, vulnerability prioritization, and user-identifying network metadata. The most security-relevant items involve malicious editor...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window centers on trust boundaries that attackers and platform operators are increasingly exploiting or redefining: developer tooling, reputation systems, vulnerability prioritization, and user-identifying network metadata. The most security-relevant items involve malicious editor extensions reaching privileged developer environments and a crypto clipper campaign using coordinated fake legitimacy signals across software-hosting and media platforms. Socket announced beta support in Socket Firewall for blocking malicious VS Code Marketplace and Open VSX extensions before they reach developer workstations. The capability routes editor marketplace requests through Socket’s proxy and filters search, install, update, and VSIX download flows based on threat verdicts, extending supply chain controls into IDE extension ecosystems. The announcement cites GitHub’s May 2026 disclosure that attackers compromised an employee device through the malicious Nx Console 18.95.0 VS Code extension and exfiltrated roughly 3,800 GitHub-internal repositories. Socket emphasized that editor extensions may access source code, terminals, files, credentials, tokens, and internal systems, making compromised extensions a high-impact intrusion path inside development environments. Check Point Research reported a crypto clipper campaign distributing a Rust-based clipboard hijacker disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots and crash-game predictors. The malware targets Windows and macOS users, monitors clipboard contents for cryptocurrency wallet address patterns, and replaces matching entries with attacker-controlled addresses from a hard-coded list. The campaign’s distribution model relies heavily on manufactured credibility. The actor used paid or promoted posts on legitimate news websites, coordinated positive comments on VirusTotal, GitHub, SourceForge, and YouTube, and inflated indicators such as stars, forks, downloads, upvotes, and AI-generated narrator content. Check Point also reported use of EIN Presswire to market the tools’ claimed capabilities, resulting in syndication across partner news sites, primarily in the USA TODAY Network. Tenable published analysis on operationalizing CISA Binding Operational Directive 26-04, which supersedes BOD 19-02 and BOD 22-01. The directive shifts federal vulnerability management away from static severity scoring and toward remediation timelines based on four contextual risk variables: asset exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities status, exploit automation, and technical impact. According to Tenable, asset exposure is the highest-leverage variable in the directive’s model. Its analysis found that removing public exposure shifted 76.7% of CVEs from compressed remediation timelines into the deferral tier. The article also notes that BOD 26-04 introduces a forensic triage requirement for KEV-listed vulnerabilities that yield total system control, and Tenable states that 83% of actively exploited CVEs meet that impact threshold. Google has begun notifying advertisers that it will start using IP addresses for ad measurement and personalization across the European Economic Area, the UK, and Switzerland on or shortly after August 3, 2026. The company said IP addresses it already receives for traffic routing and ad delivery will also be used to identify devices for measurement and personalization. The article notes that IP addresses are considered personal data under GDPR in the EEA, the UK, and Switzerland, and that using automatically transmitted information such as IP addresses to identify devices is a building block of fingerprinting. Google plans to register under IAB Europe’s Transparency and Consent Framework for Feature 3, while its customer email states that advertisers remain bound by Google’s EU User Consent Policy and must obtain valid consent from users in affected regions.

item.title: ClickFix SmartRAT and Meow Campaigns, FortiBleed Leak, and UK CNI State Threats
item.description: This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting,...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting, and renewed warnings about state prepositioning in critical infrastructure. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported a March 2026 ClickFix campaign using an AI-generated typosquatting site impersonating a Brazilian bank to deliver SmartRAT. The lure presented a fraudulent Cloudflare CAPTCHA followed by a fullscreen fake BSOD recovery prompt, pressuring victims into executing a PowerShell command that downloaded the malware. SmartRAT provides encrypted C2, remote screen and input control, keylogging, fake bank overlays, QR code interception, and persistence via scheduled tasks and a Windows service. SmartRAT is financially focused, monitoring window titles associated with Brazilian banking, payment, and cryptocurrency activity and alerting operators when victims interact with targeted services. ThreatLabz also found a weakness in the SmartRAT web C2 panel in which client-side authentication could be bypassed by setting arbitrary localStorage values. Netskope Threat Labs separately described a macOS ClickFix campaign observed on May 31, 2026 that delivered an upgraded AppleScript payload labeled “Meow (DEBUG).” The infection chain remained fileless until persistence, using a victim-executed curl command to fetch a compressed stager and pipe AppleScript into osascript memory. The payload checks for Russian keyboard input, steals credentials, cookies, keychain contents, crypto wallet data, messaging sessions, files, gaming data, and Apple Notes, then persists as com.apple.accountsd for recurring C2 beacons and arbitrary command execution. A newly identified leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed apparent Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs worldwide. Researcher Bob Diachenko said the exposed server contained usernames, email addresses, and plaintext passwords, with references to major organizations including Chevron, Samsung, Foxconn, Comcast, AT&T, Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Sinopec, and State Grid. Kevin Beaumont reported confirming the authenticity of some administrative credentials and said the dataset appeared to originate from exported Fortinet configurations. The FortiBleed reporting attributed the activity to a Russian-speaking, multi-operator threat group that allegedly made about 1.16 billion credential attempts against 320,777 FortiGate targets and 2.1 billion attempts against 163,650 Microsoft SQL Server systems. In parallel, Zscaler’s 2026 phishing reporting found that overall phishing volume in its cloud fell 20% year over year, while remaining campaigns became more targeted, AI-powered, and difficult to distinguish from legitimate activity. Zscaler identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances during the analysis period and assessed 9% as malicious. Government phishing attempts increased 50% year over year to 138.5 million hits in 2025, while healthcare saw lower phishing counts but 95.2% of phishing activity over encrypted channels. Separate account takeover reporting emphasized attacker adaptation to MFA through fatigue tactics, adversary-in-the-middle frameworks, browser-in-the-middle kits, session hijacking, and credential theft from unmanaged or compromised endpoints. Richard Horne, chief executive of the UK National Cyber Security Centre, said the agency handled more than 200 incidents affecting UK critical infrastructure and its supporting ecosystem in the year to May, with roughly 75% believed to involve state actors. He warned that adversaries are prepositioning inside technology underpinning British critical national infrastructure to enable rapid exploitation and mass disruption during conflict. Horne cited Volt Typhoon as the clearest example of this prepositioning model. The remarks coincided with UK government work on the Cyber Security and Resilience Bill and an expected National Cyber Action Plan. Horne framed cybersecurity as a contest against capable opponents rather than only a risk-management discipline, emphasizing capability relative to adversaries. Ukraine has also been granted access to the European Union’s Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing Kyiv to request EU-approved expertise during major cyberattacks. The reserve, managed by ENISA, provides incident response, digital forensics, technical expertise, and recovery support when incidents exceed national capacity. Ukrainian officials said the country already shares intelligence on Russian hacking techniques with European partners and participates in joint cybercrime investigations and attribution work. Censys analyzed AdaptixC2, an open-source C2 framework whose default configuration exposes identifiable HTTP headers such as Server: AdaptixC2 and Adaptix-Version: v1.2 on unauthenticated error responses. As of 16 June 2026, Censys tracked 412 web properties across 236 hosts using default or near-default AdaptixC2 settings. The research identified beacon listener clusters hosted by Hivelocity, M247, and PSB Hosting, as well as one host serving AdaptixC2 payloads from an open HTTP directory. The exposed directory included a Linux installer that deployed timesync.bin and established 12 persistence mechanisms. Censys noted that even operators who changed the default Server header could remain detectable if they left the default 404 response body string intact. Cato Networks reported on a French-speaking attacker known as “Poisson,” who compromised a small French automotive business, installed a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials. Before his Havoc C2 server went offline, the attacker installed OpenSSH Server and Tailscale on a victim machine to preserve access outside the original C2 channel. Cato captured 339 commands over 33 days after the operator left SSH keys and a step-by-step playbook in an open storage bucket, with the intrusion involving VBScript, PowerShell, .NET loaders, Havoc Demon, scheduled-task persistence, Explorer.exe injection, RustDesk, and a Python keylogger. Silent Push described the preparation phase as the period in which threat actors build and stage domains, servers, DNS records, certificates, and testing environments before a campaign becomes active. The company argued that SIEMs, EDR platforms, blocklists, and standard threat intelligence feeds generally rely on observed malicious events or known-bad indicators, leaving this phase underdetected. The article stated that traditional threat data captures roughly the final 5% of the offensive lifecycle, when infrastructure is already operational. Silent Push said its approach maps DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, and host configurations to identify Indicators of Future Attack, claiming an average early-detection lead time of 104 days ahead of traditional SIEM alerts and more than 300 days in some APT cases.

item.title: Mastra npm Takeover, CVE-2026-48907 Exploitation, and Crypto Hijacker Campaigns
item.description: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and productivity platforms, alongside ongoing concerns about exposure prioritization and public-sector cyber capacity. Mend reported a compromise of more than 140 packages in the @mastra npm scope, where an attacker republished packages with a new dependency on easy-day-js. Version 1.11.22 of easy-day-js introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper that retrieved a second-stage Node.js payload from 23.254.164.92:8000 and launched it as a detached hidden process communicating with 23.254.164.123:443. The second stage was described as harvesting environment secrets and cryptocurrency wallet data, with affected installations treated in the reporting as fully compromised. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address CVE-2026-48907, a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin. The JCE security team said the vulnerability allows unauthenticated PHP upload and execution, is actively exploited, has public exploit code available, and is being targeted through automated attacks. The flaw was fixed in JCE Pro 2.9.99.6, while the vendor noted that updating does not remediate sites already compromised before patching. Broader exposure data from Intruder underscored why these classes of issues remain operationally significant. Across 3,000 attack surfaces, 60% of organizations had at least one HTTP panel exposed, 42% had an internet-reachable database, and 30% exposed files or information that should not have been public. MySQL, Postgres, API documentation, WordPress admin panels, and RDP were among the most common findings. Check Point Research analyzed a Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijacker campaign disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots, Aviator Predictor tools, and crash-game predictors. The actor used a WordPress phishing site as a landing hub and promoted payloads through GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, crypto forums, and posts on legitimate news websites. The campaign also inflated trust signals using coordinated or fake GitHub engagement, SourceForge ratings and downloads, YouTube activity, and benign VirusTotal votes and comments. The Windows payload persisted by copying itself to %APPDATA%\\silke\\silke.exe and creating a Startup folder shortcut, while the macOS variant used an unlocker.command script to remove quarantine attributes and installed a LaunchAgent plist with RunAtLoad and KeepAlive. Both monitored clipboard or pasteboard contents and replaced cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses. IRONSCALES reported a French-language invoice notification sent through Mailgun from an Ecocert accounting subdomain that passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication. The email linked to an Upflow billing portal URL containing a JWT with portal.view permissions, a customer identifier, and a 60-day expiration, allowing anyone with the tokenized link to access the portal without credentials. The invoice requested a 380 euro payment and included French IBAN and BIC details, while a tracking pixel confirmed mailbox activity. BleepingComputer reported that India restricted Telegram access nationwide until June 22 after the National Testing Agency said the platform was used to sell access to leaked exam materials. The disruption extended beyond India, including to users in the UAE, after AS18101 announced Telegram prefixes and the route leaked globally via FLAG Telecom. Researchers confirmed the route hijack but said they found no evidence that the wider disruption was deliberate, while civil society groups criticized the block as disproportionate. Microsoft confirmed it is investigating Office launch issues after Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026. The issue affects third-party applications using OLE automation to open Office apps or documents, including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Access, and may fail without an error message. User reports named affected applications such as CCH Engagement, Zotero, Workpaper Manager, Dentrix, and Softdent. Operationally, The Record covered Sen. Mark Warner’s letter warning of CISA staffing cuts, short-staffed regional divisions, and the disbanding of the MS-ISAC program supporting state and local critical infrastructure operators. Separately, Netskope introduced its Catalyst MSP/SP Program for managed SSE and SASE delivery, while a Hacker News article framed adversarial exposure validation as a way to distinguish theoretical exposure from reachable and consequential risk within Continuous Threat Exposure Management.
